# Decoding the Configuration of AI Coding Agents: Insights from Claude Code Projects

## Core Thesis

Public Claude Code project instructions in this sample are organized primarily around helping the agent understand software architecture, with architecture guidance appearing far more often than other instruction types. The result is useful as a map of current configuration practice in popular public repositories, but it is a descriptive snapshot of a filtered corpus rather than a performance proof about what makes coding agents work best.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This matters because it sharpens what should count as serious agent-operating context. If high-visibility real-world projects most often spend their instruction budget on architecture, dependencies, and project overview, then Sapho should treat structural project understanding as a first-class part of agent evaluation rather than as optional prompt decoration. At the same time, the paper is a warning against cargo-culting current repository conventions into doctrine: the sample is popularity-filtered, classification-sensitive, and explicitly time-bounded in a fast-moving agent ecosystem.

## Key Findings

- The study began from 4,724 repositories containing Claude.md, then filtered to public English-language, higher-star, real-world projects and kept the top 100 repositories, ultimately analyzing 328 configuration files after replacing 45 pointer-style memory-bank files with the markdown they referenced.
- Architecture was the most common concern in the corpus, appearing in 238 files, or 72.6% of the dataset, well ahead of general development guidelines at 147 files (44.8%) and project overview at 128 files (39.0%).
- The most common recurring concern combination was Architecture, Dependencies, and Project Overview, which appeared in 21.6% of files under the study's FP-Max co-occurrence analysis with minimum support set to 0.15.
- The corpus spans mature and visible repositories rather than toy examples: the selected repositories covered 23 programming languages and had medians of 950 stars, 488 commits, and 58 months since first commit.
- The paper is methodologically useful as a field snapshot, but its section taxonomy depends in part on one author's initial manual grouping of 2,492 section titles, and the authors explicitly warn that observed patterns may shift as coding-agent practice evolves.

## Evidence and Findings

- The source shows a large funnel from 4,724 detected repositories to 100 selected repositories and then 328 analyzed files, with exclusions for fewer than 100 stars, non-English projects, and repositories judged not to be real-world applications. That supports the conclusion that the paper is studying a narrowed slice of visible public practice rather than the full Claude Code ecosystem, which matters because any downstream doctrine built from it inherits those selection bounds.
- Architecture appears in 238 of 328 files, or 72.6%, compared with 147 files for general development guidelines and 128 for project overview. That supports the conclusion that maintainers most often use these configuration artifacts to orient the agent to system structure before or alongside procedural coding advice, which matters because it suggests project topology is treated as operationally central in real deployments.
- The most common mined pattern is the trio of Architecture, Dependencies, and Project Overview at 21.6% of files. That supports the conclusion that maintainers recurrently package structural explanation, dependency context, and high-level project framing together, which matters because effective agent setup appears to rely on compound context blocks rather than a single isolated instruction category.
- The paper extracted 2,492 section titles and grouped them into semantic categories through one author's manual analysis with review by two additional authors, then ran FP-Max pattern mining with minimum support of 0.15. That supports the conclusion that the findings are a structured descriptive analysis rather than anecdotal reading, which matters because the reported frequencies and patterns are methodical enough to inform evaluation heuristics even if they do not establish causality.
- File structure in the corpus is uneven: the median file had seven level-2 headings, the maximum was 213, and 36 files had no level-2 sections, requiring analysis at level-1 instead. That supports the conclusion that Claude Code instructions are highly heterogeneous in form, which matters because any parser, evaluator, or template system that assumes a stable configuration schema will miss a large share of real-world variation.
- Rich visual structure appears limited in the sampled files: links were most frequent inside Architecture sections at only 1.83%, and the authors found just two Mermaid UML diagram instances. That supports the conclusion that most projects still communicate agent guidance in plain markdown prose rather than through diagram-heavy structured representations, which matters because current practice remains text-dominant even when architecture is the top concern.

## Contradictions and Tensions

- The strongest visible tension is between prevalence and demonstrated effectiveness. Architecture dominates the corpus at 72.6%, but the paper does not show that architecture-heavy files produce better agent outcomes, so a common practice can be highly visible without being causally validated.
- The pattern result is also method-dependent. The headline combination of Architecture, Dependencies, and Project Overview appears in 21.6% of files only under the chosen FP-Max setup and support threshold, so the apparent canonical pattern is partly a property of analytical choices rather than an invariant law of agent configuration.
- The corpus is broad in language coverage but narrow in repository selection. It spans 23 languages and relatively mature projects, yet it excludes lower-star, non-English, and non-real-world repositories, creating a tension between apparent ecosystem diversity and actual sampling narrowness.
- The study aims to map current practice, but the authors explicitly note that coding-agent behavior and project conventions may change over time. That means the paper is useful precisely as a dated snapshot, while its practical value decays if treated as timeless guidance.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The strongest bounded explanation is that repository maintainers treat agent effectiveness as dependent on situational orientation: the agent needs to know how the codebase is organized, what its major components are, and what dependencies shape safe modification before narrower coding instructions become useful. The evidence supports this as an operational reading of what maintainers write into configuration files, not as a demonstrated causal mechanism for improved coding performance. The result is bounded to public, English, popularity-filtered repositories, to files normalized through replacement of pointer-style memory-bank references, and to a category system built from manual semantic grouping. It is therefore a descriptive map of current instruction design choices, not a proof of optimal prompt architecture.

## Limits

The paper does not connect configuration content to benchmark scores, task success, defect rates, or any other direct outcome measure, so it cannot show which instruction patterns actually improve coding agents.
The category system begins with one author's manual semantic grouping, which introduces classification sensitivity even with later review.
The sample excludes large parts of the ecosystem by design, including lower-visibility and non-English repositories.
The analysis is time-bounded to a rapidly changing agent landscape, and the authors explicitly acknowledge that the observed configuration mix may drift as tools and practices evolve.

# Decoding the Configuration of AI Coding Agents: Insights from Claude Code Projects

## Core Thesis

Public Claude Code project instructions in this sample are organized primarily around helping the agent understand software architecture, with architecture guidance appearing far more often than other instruction types. The result is useful as a map of current configuration practice in popular public repositories, but it is a descriptive snapshot of a filtered corpus rather than a performance proof about what makes coding agents work best.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This matters because it sharpens what should count as serious agent-operating context. If high-visibility real-world projects most often spend their instruction budget on architecture, dependencies, and project overview, then Sapho should treat structural project understanding as a first-class part of agent evaluation rather than as optional prompt decoration. At the same time, the paper is a warning against cargo-culting current repository conventions into doctrine: the sample is popularity-filtered, classification-sensitive, and explicitly time-bounded in a fast-moving agent ecosystem.

- The study began from 4,724 repositories containing Claude.md, then filtered to public English-language, higher-star, real-world projects and kept the top 100 repositories, ultimately analyzing 328 configuration files after replacing 45 pointer-style memory-bank files with the markdown they referenced.

- The corpus spans mature and visible repositories rather than toy examples: the selected repositories covered 23 programming languages and had medians of 950 stars, 488 commits, and 58 months since first commit.
- The paper is methodologically useful as a field snapshot, but its section taxonomy depends in part on one author's initial manual grouping of 2,492 section titles, and the authors explicitly warn that observed patterns may shift as coding-agent practice evolves.

## Evidence and Findings

- The source shows a large funnel from 4,724 detected repositories to 100 selected repositories and then 328 analyzed files, with exclusions for fewer than 100 stars, non-English projects, and repositories judged not to be real-world applications. That supports the conclusion that the paper is studying a narrowed slice of visible public practice rather than the full Claude Code ecosystem, which matters because any downstream doctrine built from it inherits those selection bounds.
- Architecture appears in 238 of 328 files, or 72.6%, compared with 147 files for general development guidelines and 128 for project overview. That supports the conclusion that maintainers most often use these configuration artifacts to orient the agent to system structure before or alongside procedural coding advice, which matters because it suggests project topology is treated as operationally central in real deployments.
- The most common mined pattern is the trio of Architecture, Dependencies, and Project Overview at 21.6% of files. That supports the conclusion that maintainers recurrently package structural explanation, dependency context, and high-level project framing together, which matters because effective agent setup appears to rely on compound context blocks rather than a single isolated instruction category.
- The paper extracted 2,492 section titles and grouped them into semantic categories through one author's manual analysis with review by two additional authors, then ran FP-Max pattern mining with minimum support of 0.15. That supports the conclusion that the findings are a structured descriptive analysis rather than anecdotal reading, which matters because the reported frequencies and patterns are methodical enough to inform evaluation heuristics even if they do not establish causality.
- File structure in the corpus is uneven: the median file had seven level-2 headings, the maximum was 213, and 36 files had no level-2 sections, requiring analysis at level-1 instead. That supports the conclusion that Claude Code instructions are highly heterogeneous in form, which matters because any parser, evaluator, or template system that assumes a stable configuration schema will miss a large share of real-world variation.

- The strongest visible tension is between prevalence and demonstrated effectiveness. Architecture dominates the corpus at 72.6%, but the paper does not show that architecture-heavy files produce better agent outcomes, so a common practice can be highly visible without being causally validated.
- The pattern result is also method-dependent. The headline combination of Architecture, Dependencies, and Project Overview appears in 21.6% of files only under the chosen FP-Max setup and support threshold, so the apparent canonical pattern is partly a property of analytical choices rather than an invariant law of agent configuration.
- The corpus is broad in language coverage but narrow in repository selection. It spans 23 languages and relatively mature projects, yet it excludes lower-star, non-English, and non-real-world repositories, creating a tension between apparent ecosystem diversity and actual sampling narrowness.
- The study aims to map current practice, but the authors explicitly note that coding-agent behavior and project conventions may change over time. That means the paper is useful precisely as a dated snapshot, while its practical value decays if treated as timeless guidance.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The strongest bounded explanation is that repository maintainers treat agent effectiveness as dependent on situational orientation: the agent needs to know how the codebase is organized, what its major components are, and what dependencies shape safe modification before narrower coding instructions become useful. The evidence supports this as an operational reading of what maintainers write into configuration files, not as a demonstrated causal mechanism for improved coding performance. The result is bounded to public, English, popularity-filtered repositories, to files normalized through replacement of pointer-style memory-bank references, and to a category system built from manual semantic grouping. It is therefore a descriptive map of current instruction design choices, not a proof of optimal prompt architecture.

## Limits

The paper does not connect configuration content to benchmark scores, task success, defect rates, or any other direct outcome measure, so it cannot show which instruction patterns actually improve coding agents.
The category system begins with one author's manual semantic grouping, which introduces classification sensitivity even with later review.
The sample excludes large parts of the ecosystem by design, including lower-visibility and non-English repositories.
The analysis is time-bounded to a rapidly changing agent landscape, and the authors explicitly acknowledge that the observed configuration mix may drift as tools and practices evolve.
